elements.sent-2411-002: In this short note, we introduce cospectral graphons, paralleling the notion of cospectral graphs.
elements.sent-2411-003: As in the graph case, we give three equivalent definitions: by equality of spectra, by equality of cycle densities, and by a unitary transformation.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also give an example of two cospectral graphons that cannot be approximated by two sequences of cospectral graphs in the cut distance.
elements.sent-2411-005: There are several prominent equivalences defined on finite graphs on vertex set $[n]$ which have the following form for graphs $G$ and $H$,.
elements.sent-2411-006: For the adjacency matrix $A_G$ of $G$ and for the adjacency matrix $A_H$ of $H$ we have $TA_H=A_GT$ for a suitable $n\times n$ matrix $T\in\mathcal{M _n$, and equivalently.}
elements.sent-2411-007: We have the equality of homomorphism counts $\hom(F,G)=\hom(F,H)$ for all $F\in\mathcal{F}$.\footnote{Recall that a homomorphism of a graph $F$ to a graph $G$ is any map $h:V(F)\to V(G)$ such that $h(u)h(v)\in E(G)$ for every $uv\in E(F)$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: The symbol $\hom(F,G)$ denotes the size of the set of all homomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-009: These equivalencies are graph isomorphism, fractional isomorphism, cospectrality, and quantum isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-010: Let us give details and specify the families of matrices $\mathcal{M _n$ and of graphs $\mathcal{F}$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The most important of such equivalences is the usual graph isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-012: In that case, obviously, $\mathcal{M _n$ is the set of all $n\times n$ permutation matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The characterization of graph isomorphism using~\ref{F2 is a famous result of Lov\'asz~\cite{MR214529 , that is, for $\mathcal{F =\{\text{all graphs}\}$, the test~\ref{F2 passes only for $G$ and $H$ isomorphic.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Fractional isomorphism concept was introduced in~\cite{MR0843938 using~\ref{F1 for $\mathcal{M _n$ equal to all doubly stochastic matrices.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Later, several further equivalent definitions were given in~\cite{MR1297385 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Finally, Dvořák~\cite{MR2668548 gave a characterization as in~\ref{F2 , with $\mathcal{F}$ being all trees.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Cospectrality (sometimes called isospectrality) is derived from linear algebra, where this notion denotes equality of spectra (including multiplicities) of matrices.
elements.sent-2411-018: For graphs, this concept is applied to adjacency matrices.\footnote{Less often, some other matrices, such as the normalized Laplacian, are used.}
elements.sent-2411-019: These alternative definitions lead to different notions of cospectrality.
elements.sent-2411-020: Basic linear algebra then asserts that for a characterization using~\ref{F1 we need to take $\mathcal{M _n$ to be all orthonormal $n\times n$ matrices.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: For a characterization using~\ref{F2 we take $\mathcal{F}$ to be all cycles.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The key insight to infer the equivalence of these two definitions is that when we write $C_k$ for the cycle of length $k$, $\hom(C_k,G)$ is equal to the trace of $A_G^k$, which is in turn equal to sum of the $k$-th powers of the eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-023: Quantum isomorphism is the newest addition to the list.
elements.sent-2411-024: It was introduced in~\cite{ATSERIAS2019289 , using two definitions, of which we mention only the one in the form~\ref{F1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The class of matrices $\mathcal{M _n$ is in a sense similar to that used in fractional isomorphism.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Indeed, it is also required the entries in each row and column sum to the identity.
elements.sent-2411-027: The difference is that the entries are now not reals in $[0,1]$ but rather orthogonal projectors (say, in an $n$-dimensional complex vector space).
elements.sent-2411-028: In~\cite{quantumisomorphismequivalentequality it was shown that this definition is equivalent to~\ref{F2 with $\mathcal{F}$ being all planar graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this note we translate the concept of cospectrality to graphons.
elements.sent-2411-030: Graphons are analytic objects introduced by Borgs, Chayes, Lovász, Sós, Szegedy, and Vesztergombi~\cite{LOVASZ2006933,BORGS20081801 to represent limits of sequences of graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-031: We recall the definition and properties we shall need in Section~\ref{sec:Preliminaries .}
elements.sent-2411-032: At this moment, it suffices that each graphon is a symmetric bounded Lebesgue measurable function on $[0,1]^2$.
elements.sent-2411-033: In particular, it can be viewed as an integral kernel operator on $L^2([0,1])$.
elements.sent-2411-034: There are several directions in which graphons can be studied.
elements.sent-2411-035: Below are the two most important ones.
elements.sent-2411-036: Graphons can be studied per se, often motivated by concepts from finite graphs.
elements.sent-2411-037: As one of the most prominent examples, the seminal paper~\cite{LOVASZ2006933 introduces the notion of homomorphism density $t(F,W)$ (here, $F$ is a graph and $W$ is a graphon) which is motivated by the notion of homomorphism counts $\hom(F,G)$ (or, perhaps better, by the notion of homomorphism density $\hom(F,G)v(G)^{-v(F)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Other concepts include for example notions of max-cut~\cite{BORGS20081801 , or the chromatic number~\cite{HlaRoc:IndependentSets .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Note that there is a very easy way to introduce the counterparts of graph isomorphism, fractional isomorphism, cospectrality, or quantum isomorphism for graphons by the above characterizations using~\ref{F2 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Namely, we will say that two graphons $U$ and $W$ are (i)~weakly isomorphic, (ii)~fractionally isomorphic, (iii)~cospectral, (iv)~quantum isomorphic if $t(F,U)=t(F,W)$ for (i)~every graph~$F$, (ii)~every tree~$F$, (iii)~every cycle~$F$, (iv)~every planar graph~$F$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-042: It is now of interest to extend any further properties of these equivalence relations to graphons.
elements.sent-2411-043: As for weak isomorphism, the main result of~\cite{BoChLo:Moments asserts that two graphons $U$ and $W$ are weakly isomorphic if and only if for every $\eps>0$ there exists a unitary Koopman operator $T$ so that the operator norm of $TU-WT$ is less than $\eps$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recall that being a unitary Koopman operator amounts to the existence of a measure-preserving bijection $\pi:[0,1]\to[0,1]$ such that $(Tf)(x)=f(\pi(x))$ for every $f\in L^2([0,1])$ and $x\in[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-045: This is a clear counterpart to~\ref{F1 .\footnote{Note that we cannot achieve precisely $TU=WT$ in some situations, see Figure~7.1 in~\cite{MR3012035 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The theory of fractional isomorphism was introduced by Grebík and Rocha,~\cite{Grebik2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Specifically, \cite{Grebik2022 gives graphon counterparts to all known characterizations of graph fractional isomorphism and proves their equivalence to the above definition using tree densities.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The characterization of~\ref{F1 is $TU=WT$ for a suitable `Markov operator' $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: One of the main contributions in this note is that in Theorem~\ref{thm:spec_equiv we give several definitions of `cospectral graphons' and prove their equivalence.}
elements.sent-2411-050: As far as we know, no attempt to extend the notion of quantum isomorphism to graphons has been made.
elements.sent-2411-051: A particularly important line of research is in investigating continuity properties of various graph(on) parameters.
elements.sent-2411-052: The most prominent example is again the homomorphism density.
elements.sent-2411-053: In particular, the so-called Counting lemma asserts that if $(G_n)_n$ is a sequence of graphs converging to a graphon $W$, then for every graph $F$ we have $\hom(F,G_n)v(G_n)^{-v(F)}\to t(F,W)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: This yields positive results for the for equivalences above.
elements.sent-2411-055: Namely, suppose that $(G_n)_n$ and $(H_n)_n$ are sequences of graphs so that for each $n$, the graphs $G_n$ and $H_n$ are of the same order and isomorphic, or fractionally isomorphic, or cospectral, or quantum isomorphic.
elements.sent-2411-056: Suppose further that $(G_n)_n$ converges to a graphon $U$ and $(H_n)_n$ converges to a graphon $W$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Then by the above, $G_n$ and $H_n$ have the same counts (and thus also densities) of homomorphisms from each graph, or from each tree, or from each cycle, or from each planar graph, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-058: By the continuity of homomorphism densities, these equalities are inherited to $U$ and $W$.
elements.sent-2411-059: We conclude that $U$ and $W$ are weakly isomorphic, or fractionally isomorphic, or cospectral, or quantum isomorphic.
elements.sent-2411-060: The main result of~\cite{HlaHng:ApproximatingFIG goes in the opposite direction in the case of fractional isomorphism: If $U$ and $W$ are fractionally isomorphic then we can find sequences of graphs $G_n\to U$ and $H_n\to W$ such that for each $n$, the graphs $G_n$ and $H_n$ are fractionally isomorphic.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The second result of this note, Theorem~\ref{thm:cospectralinaproximable , is that the counterpart of this result for cospectrality does not hold.}
elements.sent-2411-062: For a graph $G$ we denote by $v(G)$ the number of vertices of $G$ and $e(G)$ the number of edges.
elements.sent-2411-063: By $C_k$ we denote cycle of length $k$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We now recall basic of the theory of graphons, using mostly notation from the excellent monograph~\cite{MR3012035 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: A graphon is a symmetric measurable function $W : [0, 1]^2 \rightarrow [0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-066: When we view the sets $[0,1]$ or $[0,1]^2$ as measure spaces, we take the underlying measure to be the Lebesgue measure.
elements.sent-2411-067: The cut-norm distance is defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:defCutNorm} d_\square(U, W) = \underset{S, T \subseteq [0,1]}{\sup}\left|\int_{S\times T} U(x, y) - W(x, y) \, dx\, dy\right|,\end{equation} where the supremum ranges over all measurable subsets $S, T$ of $[0,1]$.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq:defCutNorm} d_\square(U, W) = \underset{S, T \subseteq [0,1]}{\sup}\left|\int_{S\times T} U(x, y) - W(x, y) \, dx\, dy\right|,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-068: The cut-distance is defined as \begin{equation}\delta_\square(U, W) = \inf_\varphi d_\square(U^\varphi, W),\end{equation} where infimum ranges over all measure preserving bijections $\varphi: [0,1] \rightarrow [0,1]$ and $U^\varphi (x,y) = U(\varphi(x), \varphi(y))$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Obviously, the cut-distance is in fact a pseudodistance.
elements.sent-2411-070: Recall that each graph can be represented as a graphon.
elements.sent-2411-071: That is, if $G$ is a graph on vertex set $V$, then we define a graphon $W_G$ in the following way.
elements.sent-2411-072: Partition $[0,1]$ into sets $\{\Omega_v\}_{v\in V}$ of measure $\frac{1 {|V|}$ each.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The representation $W_G$ is not unique as it depends on the choice of the partition $\{\Omega_v\}_{v\in V}$.
elements.sent-2411-074: However any two representations are at (pseudo-)distance~0 with respect to the cut distance.
elements.sent-2411-075: The phrase sequence of graphs $(G_n)_n$ converges to $W$ which appeared several times above then means $\delta_\square(W_{G_n ,W)\to 0$ and $v(G_n)\to\infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The density of a graph $G$ in a graphon $W$ is defined as \begin{equation}t(G, W) = \int_{(x_v)_{v\in v(G)} \in [0,1]^{V(G)}} \underset{uv\in E(G)}{\prod} W(x_u,x_v)\prod_{v\in V(G)}dx_v\;.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: So far, the analogies between the adjacency matrix of a graph and a graphon were made in the original domain $[0,1]^2$.
elements.sent-2411-078: In the language of Fourier transform, this would correspond to the spatial perspective.
elements.sent-2411-079: We now move to the dual perspective.
elements.sent-2411-080: Namely, we recall formalism that allows us to study spectral properties of graphons.
elements.sent-2411-081: Details can be found in Section~7.5 of~\cite{MR3012035 .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Each graphon $W$ can be associated with an operator $T_W: L^2([0,1]) \rightarrow L^2([0,1])$ defined by \begin{equation}(T_W f)(x) = \int_0^1 W(x,y)f(y)\, dy\quad for every $x\in[0,1]$.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-083: It is known that $T_W$ is a symmetric real Hilbert-Schmidt operator.
elements.sent-2411-084: In particular, $f\in L^2([0,1])$ and $\lambda\in\mathbb{R}$ are eigenvector and eigenvalue of $W$ if $T_W f=\lambda f$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Since $T_W$ is a compact operator it has a discrete real spectrum (that is, the multiset of eigenvalues), denoted by $Spec(W)$.
elements.sent-2411-086: It is well-known that for each $\eps>0$ the number of eigenvalues of modulus at least $\eps$ (including multiplicities) is finite.
elements.sent-2411-087: Further, Parseval's Theorem (see e.g.~(7.23) in~\cite{MR3012035 ) asserts that \begin{equation}\label{eq:Parseval} \|W\|_2^2= \sum_{\lambda \in Spec(W)} \lambda^2\;.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-088: We mentioned in Section~\ref{sec:Intro that for $k\ge 3$ and a graph $G$, the quantity $\hom(C_k,G)$ is the sum of the $k$-th powers of the eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The graphon counterpart to this is (see~(7.22) in~\cite{MR3012035 ) \begin{equation}\label{eq:eigen_cycles} t(C_k, W) = \sum_{\lambda \in Spec(W)} \lambda^k\quad for each $k\ge 3$.\end{equation} Last, recall that an operator $T$ on $L^2([0,1])$ is unitary if $T$ is surjective and for every $f \in L^2([0,1])$, we have $\| T f \|_2 = \| f \|_2$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Unitary operators are functional-analytic counterparts to orthonormal matrices.
elements.sent-2411-091: We defined cospectral graphons in Section~\ref{sec:Intro .}
elements.sent-2411-092: Since not all the concepts were defined back then, we repeat the definition here.
elements.sent-2411-093: Graphons $U$ and $W$ are cospectral if and only if for all integers $k\ge 3$, $t(C_k, U) = t(C_k, W)$.
elements.sent-2411-094: In our first main theorem, we prove that graphon cospectrality has several equivalent definitions.
elements.sent-2411-095: For every two graphons $U$ and $W$, the following are equivalent:.
elements.sent-2411-096: For all integers $k$ bigger than $3$, we have $t(C_k, U) = t(C_k, W)$.
elements.sent-2411-097: There are infinitely many odd numbers $k$ and infinitely many even numbers $k$, such that $t(C_k, U) = t(C_k, W)$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Graphons $U$ and $W$ have the same spectra, that is, $Spec(U) = Spec(W)$.
elements.sent-2411-099: There exists a unitary operator $T: L^2([0,1]) \rightarrow L^2([0,1])$, such that $T \circ T_W = T_U \circ T$.
